item.title: Rice World Cup Cities Hack
item.description:
Tuesday, June 23, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Ion, 4201 Main Street, Houston, TX, 77002, United States

The Rice World Cup Cities Hack is a sustainability-focused virtual data hackathon tied to the FIFA World Cup 2026. Teams will work with real-world urban datasets to build data management and visualization solutions, analyzing the sustainability impact of the World Cup on host cities like Houston. 📅 Kick-Off Workshop: June 23-24 at The Ion, Houston – Register – https://forms.gle/uBfedtqia27Zmafe7 💻 Virtual Hackathon: June 23-September 15 🏆 $10,000 Grand Prize | $17,500 in total prizes Challenge tracks include Transportation & Access, Energy-Food-Water Nexus, Public Health & the Built Environment, and High-Intensity Corridors & Future Growth Districts. Interdisciplinary teams are encouraged! Register for Hack by June 22: https://rice-urban-sustainability.devpost.com/

From Ion District

item.title: Wellness Walk
item.description:
Tuesday, June 23, 2026 at 6:30 PM

Location: TX

Join Laura Conely of Urban Paths for a fun evening as we explore the trails and beauty of Hermann Park on a guided 3-mile experience. All are invited to this conversationally paced walk; friendly pups on leash are welcome too! This event is free, but registration is required and space is limited. Sign up HERE. Participants will meet outside the McGovern Centennial Gardens, located at 1500 Hermann Drive, with parking available in adjacent Lot C. Laura Conely is the founder of Urban Paths, a walking wellness company. As a certified teacher and coach, she leads guided Wellness Walks that bring individuals of all ages and fitness levels together to experience the profound health benefits of: Nature, Art, Movement, and Connection.

From Hermann Park Conservancy

item.title: Pitch in at Hermann Park
item.description:
Wednesday, June 24, 2026 at 8:30 AM

Location: Lake Plaza

Give the Park you love a little extra care, and help us get ready for the world! Join us for “Pitch in at Hermann Park,” a hands-on volunteer series where community members of all ages come together to help care for Houston’s most cherished green spaces. We’re proud to work alongside FWC26Houston and Harris County–Houston Sports Authority in the Impact Houston 26 efforts to elevate the local environmental action on a global stage and build a lasting, positive legacy for our city. You’ll work alongside Conservancy staff on simple but meaningful projects like mulching, planting, and litter cleanup — the kind of small efforts that make a big impact for visitors, wildlife, and the health of the Park. This volunteer session will be in the Heart of the Park (6104 Hermann Park Drive, Houston Texas 77030). Volunteers will meet at the Pedal Boat Kiosk. Enter through Lot G (near the Zoo) or Lot E and park in Lot F.  Walk up the concrete sidewalk to the Pedal Boat Kiosk, and we will be watching for your group. No experience is needed — just comfortable clothes, closed-toe shoes, and a willingness to help. We’ll provide the tools, guidance, and a great way to spend your morning outdoors while meeting new people and giving back to the community. Whether you come with friends, family, coworkers, or on your own, your time truly matters here. Help us welcome the world to Hermann Park! SIGN UP HERE. Weather:   Volunteer projects are rain or shine, unless there is severe weather. Cancellations due to severe weather will be sent via text/email at least 1 hour before your shift. Use your best judgment when traveling in inclement weather. Questions? Email volunteer@hermannpark.org or call 713-524-5876. Remember, we are often out in the field, so email is the preferred method of getting in touch!

From Hermann Park Conservancy

item.title: Free Watch Party: Czechia vs. Mexico
item.description:
Wednesday, June 24, 2026 at 6:30 PM

Two proud soccer nations with everything to play for. Czechia brings the grit and heart of Eastern Europe while Mexico fights for glory on home turf. This group stage showdown is going to be electric, so come pick a side and make some noise! Festival gates open at 6:30 p.m. to the sounds of DJ Cris. Kickoff is at 8:00 p.m. Admission is free! Food and Beverage Concessions: Enjoy a variety of festival food fare, beer and wine, and non-alcoholic beverages from Coca-Cola. Visit Smalls Sliders for burgers, fries, and shakes and cool down with Frios Gourmet Pops. Stop by Hearsay’s festival tent for a cold beer or beer bucket or grab a michelada, wine, or beer from Gaspacho’s festival tent. Non-alcoholic drinks from Coca-Cola as well as pre-packaged snacks can be purchased at the Community Partner Concessions Booth, proceeds benefiting Levy Park and the event’s community partners. Face Painting & Henna Art: Get into the tournament spirit with face and body art from Butterfly Faces by Shondra Macklin and Henna Station & Glitter Bar Community Partners and Volunteers: Levy Park is representing a wide array of non-profit park partners for this event. All sales generated at the Community Partner Concessions booth will support Levy Park’s maintenance and operation. Email us to learn more about volunteer opportunities. Merchandise: Event merchandise celebrating Houston, Levy Park, and the global soccer tournament will also be available for purchase. Art Installations and Photo Opportunities: Commemorate the experience with a photo tour of the park, including life-sized soccer-themed photo backdrops and a tournament bracket. Visit our soccer ball music box and turn the crank for festive sounds. Festival Guidelines: VISITORS MUST COMPLY WITH DIRECTIONS FROM PARK PERSONNEL, OR THEY WILL BE ASKED TO LEAVE THE PARK. PARENTS ARE RESPONSIBLE FOR THEIR CHILDREN’S SAFETY IN THE PARK. CHAIRS AND SEATING: No outside furniture. Blankets may be brought into the Festival site for the designated blanket seating area. All other furniture is available on a first-come, first-served basis. STROLLERS & WAGONS: Oversized strollers and wagons may need to be parked in a designated area during the festival. All visitors must comply with park personnel regarding stroller and wagon parking. FOOD AND BEVERAGES: Guests are permitted to enter with one factory-sealed bottle of water. Outside food and drinks are prohibited (exceptions will be made for baby food and necessary medical dietary food items). A variety of food, non-alcoholic drinks, beer, and wine will be available for purchase from Festival concessionaires. RE-ENTRY: If you leave the Festival site, you will be re-screened before re-entry. THE FOLLOWING ARE PROHIBITED: Smoking or Vaping Umbrellas Drug Use Kites and Drones Recreational Motorized Vehicles Guns, knives, any weaponry Soliciting Bikes, scooters, rollerblades, and roller skates

From Levy Park Houston

item.title: NASA Tech Talks: NASA Education Highlights
item.description:
Thursday, June 25, 2026 at 5 PM

Location: Ion, 4201 Main Street, Houston, TX, 77002, United States

We want HOU! Join us to discover NASA’s toughest tech challenges—and apply your skills and ex-pertise to solve them.This month we’re going to focus on educational opportunities offered by NASA for high school, undergraduate, and graduate students, plus partnership opportunities for colleges and universities!Information booths and activities for younger students will be available starting at 5pm, so please join us to learn more about the Education opportunities offered by NASA and how you can get in-volved!Grownups – please join us after the talk for drinks and networking at Second Draught! About the Speakers A Louisiana native, Dr. Elicia “Dynae” Fullwood was the valedictorian of her senior high school class. She received a Bachelor of Science degree in Biology from the University of Houston main campus in Houston, Texas and a Master of Education degree with specialties in Secondary Education and Education Administration from the University of St. Thomas in Houston, Texas.  She earned an Education Specialist degree from Nova Southeastern University in Miami, FL while studying Instructional Technology and Distance Education.  She earned a Doctoral degree in Education from Oklahoma State University in Stillwater, Oklahoma.  She holds a Principal certificate for K-12 School Administration in the state of Texas, an Online Teaching certificate, and a Classroom Teacher certificate for Secondary Biology in Texas. Dynae is currently working at the Lyndon B. Johnson Space Center in Houston, TX where she is the Team Lead for the Office of STEM Engagement, the organization responsible for STEM education and outreach in support of NASA’s missions. Prior to this, Dr. Fullwood led national K12 STEM Education activities related to NASA’s Artemis program. Dr. Fullwood worked as an Education Specialist with NASA’s Teaching from Space Office. Her career also lends experience as an Aerospace Education Specialist at NASA Headquarters, NASA Goddard Space Flight Center and NASA Langley Research Center. Earlier on, she taught high school science in the Houston Independent School District of Houston, Texas at James Madison High School and the School for Meteorology and Space Science. Dr. Fullwood completed the 2011 Shamrock Marathon and the 2006 Honolulu Marathon. She is a member of Alpha Kappa Alpha Sorority, Inc. Her personal hobbies include singing, telling funny stories, crocheting, learning French and being the best at taking care of her school-aged son, golden-aged parents, and the cutest Yorkshire terrier on planet Earth. Keya T. Briscoe is the Director of JSC University Engagements within NASA’s University Partnerships & Collaboration Office. With over 20 years of professional experience, Keya brings deep expertise in program management, stakeholder engagement, and strategic relationship building across academic, government, and technical communities.  Keya plays a pivotal role in strengthening academic partnerships and supporting initiatives that connect leading academic talent with NASA’s mission needs. Known for a collaborative, people‑centered approach, Keya is committed to creating pathways that expand opportunity, elevate institutional partnerships, and enhance NASA’s broader engagement strategy. Born and raised in Baltimore, Maryland, Keya earned a Bachelor’s degree in International Business from Loyola University Maryland and a Master’s degree in Human Resources Development from Towson University. Jennifer Scott Williams is the Deputy Director of the Business Development and Technology Integration Office (BDTI) at NASA’s Johnson Space Center (JSC), where she helps lead strategic planning, workforce oversight, and integration of technical, mission support, and communications activities across key areas including open innovation, partnership formulation, technology integration, technology transfer, and business integration. Born in Oklahoma and raised as an Air Force dependent, Jennifer is a dual graduate of Spelman College and Georgia Tech with bachelor’s degrees in mathematics and electrical engineering.  She began her NASA career as a Space Shuttle INCO flight controller, earning eight certifications and supporting 24 missions. She later completed a Master of Electrical Engineering at the University of Houston and went on to serve in a variety of leadership roles, including managing MUREP programs in the JSC Office of Education, leading communications operations for Boeing’s Commercial Crew Starliner, and overseeing research portfolios and payload integration for the International Space Station (ISS) Program. Selected as Branch Chief of the Applications Client Support Office in 2020, she led teams enabling technology demonstrations, STEM initiatives, commercial payloads, and external payload operations aboard the ISS. Most recently, she served a year-long detail as Technical Assistant for Integration supporting Center Director Vanessa Wyche. Today, she continues advancing human exploration through capability development, technology integration, and collaborative innovation with both traditional and emerging space partners. Glenn Johnson has 22 years of experience at Johnson Space Center training astronauts for the Space Shuttle and the ISS and helping develop equipment for the space program.  Most of this training and equipment development has been in relation to habitability for the crew–sleep quarters, food prep, cleaning, toilet. He is currently working in the HUNCH (High schools students United with NASA to Create Hardware) program for the Design and Prototype program where he gets high school students to design and build equipment for the space program as we move to returning to the moon and prepare for missions to Mars.  Before working for the space program he was a high school physics teacher and an engineer on a high-speed passenger project. Alli Westover is a graduate of Texas A&M University with a B.S. in Biomedical Engineering in 1996.   After graduation started working at NASA as an integration engineer for life science research on the Space Shuttle and for the astronauts aboard the MIR (Russian Space Station).  Performed biomedical flight readiness and training of many astronauts while working in the Cardiovascular Lab.  When my children, who are now 27 and 24, were little I left NASA for a little bit and wrote a children’s book, called ‘My Daddy Works at NASA’.  I would go into the schools and do small science experiments with the students.  I then went and taught Engineering Design at a local high-school and started working on NASA HUNCH projects in my class.  After 5 years, I joined the NASA team again and started working for the NASA HUNCH (High-school students United with NASA to Create Hardware) program.  I began the NASA HUNCH Culinary and Nutrition Science Program 11 years ago and this program has grown by leaps and bounds.  The NASA Program and the Astronauts are loving the unique foods that the students are making.   I have also been our certification engineer for all of our hardware that flies to the ISS.  In 2022 I started a new program for Health and Biomedical Engineering projects.  This program is taking real problems that our NASA flight surgeons and engineers are facing with our future endeavors to the moon and beyond.  I have been with the NASA HUNCH program now for 13 years.  Donald M. Bennett has more than 38 years of experience as an educator in Industrial Technology. Born in East Texas, he graduated from Sam Houston State University in 1981 before building a career at the intersection of classroom instruction and real-world engineering. Beginning in 2004, Mr. Bennett began participation in NASA’s HUNCH program which engages high school students in building, designing, and building hardware for the International Space Station with his students creating training UOP’s. He joined the NASA HUNCH team as Flight Configuration Manager in 2019, overseeing student-designed projects with CAD prototypes and the creation of drawings needed for the manufacture of hardware. This included on-orbit projects such as the Galley Table Systems, Tape Dispenser, EVA tools, HUNCH Utility Bracket, an ARISS Fan Cover, and the Ball Clamp monopod. Donald now brings this blend of educational leadership and NASA HUNCH program experience to engineering design programs to teachers and students in the Flight Configuration program. Montgomery B. Goforth   has more than 40 years of experience as both Engineer and Manager in a variety of highly technical space and defense-related efforts.  After working for a small defense contractor specializing in anti-submarine warfare, he joined NASA’s Johnson Space Center in 1990.  He has held leadership positions in multiple JSC Directorates and Program Offices. In his previous role as Assistant Director of the JSC Engineering Directorate, he established a framework to better focus Engineering’s efforts towards Human Spaceflight technology and systems development consistent with JSC and Agency goals for exploration. Mr. Goforth is now serving as Acting Director of the Business Development & Technology Integration Office at JSC, and as the JSC Chief Technologist.  In these capacities he will be helping to expand the Houston Aerospace Community through the identification of opportunities to develop space/terrestrial technologies of interest to JSC and Human Space Exploration.  Mr. Goforth was born in East Texas, raised in Southern California and Arizona, and graduated from Rice University (BSME 1982, MSME 1987). About NASA Tech Talks In these presentations, occurring the fourth Thursdays of the month, Mr. Montgomery Goforth and other aerospace subject matter experts will discuss the technology development challenges faced by NASA’s Johnson Space Center and the surrounding Aerospace community in our ongoing efforts as the hub of human spaceflight. Presentations will focus on the ways in which these challenges, and the associated opportunities, can be leveraged by Houston’s innovation community. We will continue to identify potential space/terrestrial technologies of interest to Human Spaceflight.  Past NASA Tech Talks have included topics such as: From Space to Startup: Leveraging NASA Technology and Innovation to Propel Your Growing Enterprise Disruptive Filter Technology for Spacecraft Water Treatment:  The New Promise of Graphene Lunar dust capture using novel multiplexed inertial filter Lunar Passive Fuel Cell Technologies: Microbes in Space Extra-Vehicular Activity (EVA) Systems Virtual/Augmented Reality in Human Spaceflight Training & Execution Human Health & Performance / Human Systems Research NASA Intellectual Property suitable for commercialization by small/start-up companies Orion’s parachutes and risk identification and mitigation strategies Leveraging Radio Frequency Identification (RFID) technology for ultra-long-lived wireless sensors

From Ion District

item.title: Efficient Automatic Data Placement for GPU and CPU Tiered Memory Systems
item.description:
Thursday, June 25, 2026 at 7 PM

Location: Duncan Hall

  Speaker: Ziyi Zhao Doctoral Candidate Thesis Defense Department: Computer Science Location: Duncan Hall 3076 Modern applications, from AI and machine learning (ML) to large-scale analytics, are increasingly data-intensive, and their performance depends on how fast they can access their data. They run on GPUs and CPUs that access that data in memory, and that memory consists of different kinds, so it forms tiers: fast but small on top, large but slow below. So where these programs' data sits in this hierarchy directly shapes how fast they run. But the fast tier is far too small to hold all the data they touch; only a small fraction is worth keeping there, and that fraction changes from phase to phase. Every page moved up forces another down, so the problem is to decide which data is worth more in the fast tier than the data it would displace, constantly as the workload runs. Making these decisions automatically is crucial but challenging. The operating system has only a coarse, indirect view of how a program accesses memory, so it must infer which data is hot; and acting on that inference is a trade-off, since the gain from better placement must outweigh the cost of the move. The key is an adaptive policy: one that turns the coarse access signal into data-movement decisions and refines itself from their feedback, learning the right placement for each workload as it runs. This thesis realizes such policies to achieve efficient data placement across three tiered-memory settings in modern systems. On the GPU, where a fast high-bandwidth memory is backed by a large, slower host memory, hardware access counters with an adaptive threshold and robust host-side management migrate hot data without thrashing, running 3 to 4x faster than the default placement on ML training and inference workloads. On the CPU, the counter-driven, adaptive placement extends to local and CXL memory. And across persistent memory and disk, the system moves data in a way that is resilient to power failures, improving system-wide performance by 1.7x. In all three settings, the policies are self-tuning, require no application changes, and generalize across AI, ML, and HPC workloads, leading in performance among OS-level approaches.https://riceuniversity.zoom.us/j/92921253250?pwd=SKtzuOREw2NqDuaqtW9NqBwFzm85fa.1 (https://riceuniversity.zoom.us/j/92921253250?pwd=SKtzuOREw2NqDuaqtW9NqBwFzm85fa.1) (Department : Computer Science)

From Rice University - Open to the Public

Event details

item.title: AI Risks: Scams & Self-Harm with Russell Klein
item.description:
Thursday, June 25, 2026 at 7 PM

Location: Herzstein Foundation Multipurpose Room

Powerful AI is now available via websites, apps and voice assistants. This workshop addresses the gaps between what these system do and the unsafe ways people commonly engage with them. Weaponization: How AI is used in scams, impersonation and pressure tactics. Fake media: Fake images and recordings, shared by friends and family, increasingly misrepresents the world we live in. Excessive trust: Why trusting advice from an AI chatbot is a bad idea, and how to do your research more safely. Mental Health: How AI chatbots and articulate-sounding, robotic voices are today leading the public to self-harm. There is no charge for this tech lecture presented by Russell Klein, RK Media Services. You must RSVP to register.

item.title: Behavioral Acculturation and Acculturative Stress in Latino Immigrant Youth: The Moderating Role of Perceived Autonomy
item.description:
Friday, June 26, 2026 at 3:30 PM

  Speaker: Valentina Maza Masters Candidate Thesis Defense Department: Psychology Latino immigrant youth represent a rapidly growing demographic in the United States, facing unique socio-ecological demands as they navigate the post-migration landscape. While research has extensively examined post-migration stressors, the psychological conditions surrounding the pre-migration phase remain underexplored. This study investigated the relationship between behavioral acculturation and acculturative stress among recently immigrated Latino youth and examined whether perceived autonomy in the migration moderates this association. Utilizing a mixed-methods design, data were analyzed from a sample of 90 Latino youth (ages 15–25, M = 19.72) recruited from a specialized public high school for recent immigrants in Houston, Texas. Participants completed the Short Acculturation Scale for Hispanics (SASH), the Societal, Attitudinal, Familial, and Environmental Acculturative Stress Scale for Children (SAFE-C), and the semi-structured Migration Experiences Interview (MEI). Hierarchical multiple regression analyses revealed that behavioral acculturation did not significantly predict acculturative stress, and the hypothesized moderation effect of perceived autonomy was not supported (p = .411). However, participant gender emerged as a powerful, persistent independent predictor of acculturative stress (B = 9.577, p = .027), with female youth reporting significantly higher stress levels (M = 42.03) than their male peers (M = 32.65).   (Department : Psychology)

From Rice University - Open to the Public

Event details

item.title: Exploring Organic Transformations with a Photocatalytic Twist
item.description:
Friday, June 26, 2026 at 3:30 PM

Location: BioScience Research Collaborative

  Speaker: Michael Yasuda Masters Candidate Thesis Defense Department: Chemistry Location: BioScience Research Collaborative 801 The use of metal catalysis has revolutionized synthesis of important molecules including natural products, pharmaceuticals, and petrochemicals. While synthetic chemists have made great strides in understanding what metal catalysis can offer, both in terms of mechanistic understanding and possible reactions, these advances have been most notable for noble metals such as palladium and rhodium. By contrast, the catalytic use and mechanistic understanding of economical and earth abundant metals remains relatively underdeveloped, hampering the effective use of these promising species in synthesis. Herein, efforts towards understanding the potential of Earth abundant metals to achieve useful synthetic transformations and hypothesis-driven fundamental study of their underlying mechanisms is disclosed. First, contributions towards a cobalt-catalyzed epoxide rearrangement with complementary selectivity to the Meinwald rearrangement, furnishing ketone products, is investigated. In a parallel study, mechanistic work towards understanding an iron-photocatalyzed decarboxylative addition across bicyclo[1.1.0]butanes (BCB), establishing the radical character of the reaction and identifying the rate determining character of the addition step via kinetic isotope effect (KIE) study, is shown. Finally, preliminary efforts towards using tetrabutylammonium decatungstate (TBADT) as a photocatalyst for various transformations is reported, including forming acyl azides from aldehydes.   https://riceuniversity.zoom.us/j/95407797129?pwd=1LTPids9WkwaUEXK8g6RFrhavaEaHR.1 Meeting ID: 954 0779 7129 Passcode: 482061 (Department : Chemistry)

item.title: Efficient Adaptation of Foundation Models for Domain-Specific Deployments
item.description:
Friday, June 26, 2026 at 6 PM

Location: Duncan Hall

  Speaker: Abhinav Jain Doctoral Candidate Thesis Defense Department: Computer Science Location: Duncan Hall DH 3076 Foundation models have demonstrated remarkable generalisation capabilities; however, transitioning them from broad pre-training to rigorous, domain-specific deployment exposes severe systemic bottlenecks. This dissertation addresses four critical challenges limiting their utility in specialised domains: insufficient grounding in formal domains, the prohibitive computational overhead of concurrently adapting to multiple domains, the inability to iteratively learn from experience, and the context constraints associated with high-dimensional multimodal perception. To systematically overcome these challenges, the dissertation presents complementary mechanisms spanning the model lifecycle, each targeting a distinct bottleneck in efficient domain-specific deployment.   First, immediately following pre-training, this work introduces a coarse-tuning stage via Reinforcement Learning with Coordinated Feedback (RLCF). This stage strictly grounds the model in the syntactic and semantic rules of formal domains without requiring costly verification of generated responses through domain-specific logical evaluators. Second, transitioning to the fine-tuning phase, the research tackles the server-side memory bottlenecks inherent in customising these grounded models for millions of concurrent users. By formulating Low-Rank Prompt Adaptation (LoPA), the computational and storage burdens of scaling highly personalised, domain-specific deployments are significantly mitigated. Third, shifting to active inference in domains with dynamic environments, the dissertation details RAG-Modulo. This framework facilitates continuous, post-deployment learning by constructing an experiential memory of model-environment interactions and formal verifier feedback, enabling the model to iteratively refine its decision-making. Finally, as environments and experiential memories expand to include high-dimensional visual data, standard uniform frame sampling quickly exhausts the context limits of foundation models, rendering them ineffective for long videos. To overcome this inference bottleneck, SIFT (Selective Indexing for Filtered Temporalities), a video indexing framework, is proposed. By dynamically sampling from a query-conditioned index, SIFT isolates highly relevant temporal evidence, enabling systems to filter extensive multimodal contexts while strictly adhering to computational budgets. Zoom Link: https://riceuniversity.zoom.us/j/3022885688?pwd=ZmdvR3E1VzM2R0FmeWtncG80WTY3dz09&omn=91581333025 (Department : Computer Science)

From Rice University - Open to the Public

item.title: Elucidating the Role of Lipid Homeostasis in Organellar Surveillance and Innate Immunity
item.description:
Friday, June 26, 2026 at 8 PM

Location: Keck Hall

  Speaker: Lois Armendariz Doctoral Candidate Thesis Defense Department: BioSciences-Biochemistry and Cell Biology Location: Keck Hall 102 In response to constant homeostatic threats, organisms have developed complex regulatory networks to monitor cellular functions and restore homeostasis. Mitochondrial dysfunction can lead to metabolic impairment, neurodegenerative and cardiovascular disease, cancer, and sensitivity to bacterial infections. Therefore, understanding these defenses is crucial for improving human health across the lifespan. Our lab has highlighted the importance of the Ethanol and Stress Response (ESRE) mitochondrial surveillance pathway, which plays a pivotal role in response to mitochondrial damage as well as Pseudomonas aeruginosa liquid-based pathogenesis (LK-Pa). I identified MDT-15 and its effectors, the fatty acid desaturases FAT-5, FAT-6, and FAT-7, as activators of the ESRE pathway. I further characterized the relationship between ESRE and lipid metabolism by demonstrating that box C/D snoRNPs regulate ESRE activation through post-transcriptional regulation of FAT-6 and FAT-7. Additionally, I uncovered an unanticipated relationship between ESRE surveillance and mitophagy by showing that suppression of ESRE leads to accumulation of the mitophagic activator, PINK-1. My results provide insight into a novel interplay among box C/D snoRNPs, MDT-15, and fatty acids in regulating mitochondrial surveillance and mitophagy. I also investigated the context-based differences in host response to P. aeruginosa liquid and agar-based pathogenesis (LK-Pa vs SK-Pa). I discovered that LK-Pa triggers HLH-30-dependent host metabolic rewiring by repressing MXL-3 nuclear localization. Additionally, I found that LK-Pa induces a major depletion of host lipid droplet content. I further characterized this lipid-mediated response by identifying 17 lipid genes crucial for mounting the host defense against LK-Pa. Lastly, I demonstrate that these lipid metabolism genes are also required for defense against liquid-based pathogenesis by Gram-positive pathogens such as Enterococcus faecalis and Staphylococcus aureus. Further investigation into these host metabolism changes will provide insight into how organisms respond to acute infection.   (Department : BioSciences-Biochemistry and Cell Biology)

From Rice University - Open to the Public

item.title: Fourth Saturday Phenology Walk
item.description:
Saturday, June 27, 2026 at 9 AM

Fourth Saturday of the Month9:00 – 11:00 amFree, donations appreciatedAll ages, minors must be accompanied by an adult Meet in front of the Nature Center building Discover the hidden seasonal rhythms of nature firsthand on our guided Phenology Walks, where we track the life cycles of different plants across ecosystems, from the first spring bud burst to winter dormancy. You’ll learn how shifting weather patterns and climate change influence the timing of these important events in our local ecosystems and how you can become a community scientist, contributing vital data from your own backyard to a global research initiative. These relaxing morning walks are led by scientists from Rice University and staff from the Arboretum. We welcome nature enthusiasts of all ages, however kids 12 years and younger must be accompanied by a guardian. Learn more about the study of phenology and ways you can contribute at https://www.usanpn.org/nn Community Science Partner

From Houston Arboretum & Nature Center

item.description:

Please dress for the weather and wear walking shoes. sold out

item.title: Free Watch Party: Round of 32 (2A vs 2B)
item.description:
Sunday, June 28, 2026 at 12:30 PM

The group stage is done and the knockout rounds are here! Thirty-two teams still standing, all of them hungry, none of them ready to go home. Tonight the tournament gets real. Join us as the first teams punch their tickets to the Round of 16. Festival gates open at 12:30 p.m. to the music of DJ Cris. Kickoff is at 2:00 p.m. Admission is free! Food and Beverage Concessions: Enjoy a variety of festival food fare, beer and wine, and non-alcoholic beverages from Coca-Cola. Visit Smalls Sliders for burgers, fries, and shakes and cool down with Frios Gourmet Pops. Stop by Hearsay’s festival tent for a cold beer or beer bucket or grab a michelada, wine, or beer from Gaspacho’s festival tent. Non-alcoholic drinks from Coca-Cola as well as pre-packaged snacks can be purchased at the Community Partner Concessions Booth, proceeds benefiting Levy Park and the event’s community partners. Community Partner: We are proud to be joined by our Community Partner, Dynamo & Dash Charities, for this event! By leveraging the passion of players, staff, and fans, Dynamo & Dash Charities provides resources and education to positively impact lives in the community. Stop by the Community Partner Booth to support their amazing work and learn more about their initiatives. All food & beverage proceeds from the Community Partner booth will support Dynamo & Dash Charities and Levy Park. Face Painting & Henna Art: Get into the tournament spirit with face and body art from Butterfly Faces by Shondra Macklin and Henna Station & Glitter Bar Merchandise: Event merchandise celebrating Houston, Levy Park, and the global soccer tournament will also be available for purchase. Art Installations and Photo Opportunities: Commemorate the experience with a photo tour of the park, including life-sized soccer-themed photo backdrops and a tournament bracket. Visit our soccer ball music box and turn the crank for festive sounds. Festival Guidelines: VISITORS MUST COMPLY WITH DIRECTIONS FROM PARK PERSONNEL, OR THEY WILL BE ASKED TO LEAVE THE PARK. PARENTS ARE RESPONSIBLE FOR THEIR CHILDREN’S SAFETY IN THE PARK. CHAIRS AND SEATING: No outside furniture. Blankets may be brought into the Festival site for the designated blanket seating area. All other furniture is available on a first-come, first-served basis. STROLLERS & WAGONS: Oversized strollers and wagons may need to be parked in a designated area during the festival. All visitors must comply with park personnel regarding stroller and wagon parking. FOOD AND BEVERAGES: Guests are permitted to enter with one factory-sealed bottle of water. Outside food and drinks are prohibited (exceptions will be made for baby food and necessary medical dietary food items). A variety of food, non-alcoholic drinks, beer, and wine will be available for purchase from Festival concessionaires. RE-ENTRY: If you leave the Festival site, you will be re-screened before re-entry. THE FOLLOWING ARE PROHIBITED: Smoking or Vaping Umbrellas Drug Use Kites and Drones Recreational Motorized Vehicles Guns, knives, any weaponry Soliciting Bikes, scooters, rollerblades, and roller skates

From Levy Park Houston

Event details

item.title: Houston Museum of Natural Science
item.description:
Sunday, June 28, 2026 at 2 PM

Location: Levy Park (Event Pavilion), TX

The Houston Museum of Natural Science comes to Levy Park to offer an opportunity to learn firsthand from Museum experts. Encounter real specimens and artifacts from the museum collection in an intimate, educational atmosphere. May 31: Rocks and Minerals Explore the fascinating characteristics of the most important building blocks of Earth’s crust. June 28: Ocean Life Dive into Earth’s largest ecosystem and examine intriguing examples of diverse ocean life. July 26: Energy Exciting demonstrations explore the remarkable forms of energy we encounter every day. August 30: Dinosaur Discovery Peek into the thrilling daily life of the dinosaur: investigate what dinosaurs ate, how they hunted, and the adaptations they developed to survive in a harsh prehistoric world. September 27: Birds Encounter an amazing variety of birds and discover their fascinating ancestral roots, while learning about their adaptations for success. October 25: Ancient Egypt From mummies to the pyramids, experience amazing architectural and cultural aspects of Ancient Egypt. November 29: Little Bodies, Big Jobs! Brings a selection of live arthropods from the Cockrell Butterfly Center to your classroom. Audiences of all ages will have a unique opportunity to meet some of the hardest workers in the invertebrate world up close and personal. December 13:  Astronomy  Investigate our place in space and ways we study this exciting place full of planets, moons, stars, and galaxies.

From Levy Park Houston

item.description:
Tuesday, June 30, 2026 at 8:30 AM

Location: Industrious, 4201 Main St, Houston, TX, 77002

Register here: https://luma.com/lpkh8qjc ​Spend the day working from Industrious at the ion. ​Join us for a free coworking day and experience what a better workday can feel like. Bring your laptop, settle in, enjoy coffee and refreshments, and get your work done in a space designed for focus, comfort, and connection. ​Whether you’re working solo, meeting a colleague, or looking for a new home base for your team, this is an easy way to experience Industrious firsthand. ​During the day, you’ll have access to: ​• Thoughtfully designed shared workspace • Coffee, breakfast, and snacks • Comfortable common areas • On-site support from our local team • Opportunities to connect with other professionals in the space ​As well as our Grant Writing Workshop! ​Industrious offers coworking, private offices, suites, meeting rooms, and flexible workplace options for individuals and teams. With 250+ locations across 80+ cities globally, we make it easier to find a workspace that works for you. Space is limited. RSVP to reserve your spot. ​Parking: Parking is free for the first 3 hours, then $10 for the remainder of the day. ​We look forward to welcoming you!

item.title: Pitch in at Hermann Park
item.description:
Wednesday, July 1, 2026 at 8:30 AM

Location: Lake Plaza

Give the Park you love a little extra care, and help us get ready for the world! Join us for “Pitch in at Hermann Park,” a hands-on volunteer series where community members of all ages come together to help care for Houston’s most cherished green spaces. We’re proud to work alongside FWC26Houston and Harris County–Houston Sports Authority in the Impact Houston 26 efforts to elevate the local environmental action on a global stage and build a lasting, positive legacy for our city. You’ll work alongside Conservancy staff on simple but meaningful projects like mulching, planting, and litter cleanup — the kind of small efforts that make a big impact for visitors, wildlife, and the health of the Park. This volunteer session will be in the Heart of the Park (6104 Hermann Park Drive, Houston Texas 77030). Volunteers will meet at the Pedal Boat Kiosk. Enter through Lot G (near the Zoo) or Lot E and park in Lot F.  Walk up the concrete sidewalk to the Pedal Boat Kiosk, and we will be watching for your group. No experience is needed — just comfortable clothes, closed-toe shoes, and a willingness to help. We’ll provide the tools, guidance, and a great way to spend your morning outdoors while meeting new people and giving back to the community. Whether you come with friends, family, coworkers, or on your own, your time truly matters here. Help us welcome the world to Hermann Park! SIGN UP HERE. Weather:   Volunteer projects are rain or shine, unless there is severe weather. Cancellations due to severe weather will be sent via text/email at least 1 hour before your shift. Use your best judgment when traveling in inclement weather. Questions? Email volunteer@hermannpark.org or call 713-524-5876. Remember, we are often out in the field, so email is the preferred method of getting in touch!

From Hermann Park Conservancy

item.title: Overcoming barriers to point-of-care nucleic acid amplification testing
item.description:
Wednesday, July 1, 2026 at 6 PM

Location: BioScience Research Collaborative

In this thesis I address aspects of three key bottlenecks limiting clinical translation of isothermal nucleic acid amplification tests (isothermal NAATs) – (1) sample preparation, (2) assay integration and clinical validation, and (3) implementation analysis in real-world use environments. Addressing these bottlenecks is important to increase clinical access to highly sensitive and specific nucleic acid diagnostics where laboratory testing is unavailable. Currently, most people globally cannot access NAAT tests when they are needed. Isothermal NAATs, which operate at a single temperature reducing instrumentation requirements and processing time, have been developed to address this need. Despite substantial research and development on isothermal NAATs for many indications, very few have achieved widespread clinical adoption. To address the need for simple sample preparation suitable to the point-of-care, I focused on extraction-free sample preparation and developed a framework to guide the design and validation of extraction-free sample preparation methods based on gaps identified in the literature (Chapter 2 and 3). This framework focuses on identifying potential amplification inhibitors, determining the lytic efficiency of different methods, and creating appropriate model samples. This framework can be used by test developers to co-design an amplification assay and suitable extraction-free sample preparation workflow. When I then applied this framework in Chapter 3 to design an extraction-free sample preparation workflow for an assay detecting β-globin from buccal swabs and blood, I was able to design extraction-free sample preparation that consistently detected target from both buccal swabs and blood. I then applied the framework I developed in Chapters 4 and 5 to optimize extraction-free sample preparation for assays for two indications. In Chapter 4, to address the need for assay integration and clinical validation, I optimized SCD-RPA, an isothermal NAAT to diagnose sickle cell anemia and sickle cell trait. I integrated the assay with a modified version of the previously developed sample preparation workflow and validated it in a multi-country study with 161 patient samples collected in both a high- and a low-resource setting. SCD-RPA demonstrated robust performance with an overall genotype prediction accuracy of 96.9%. In Chapter 5, I applied the framework for extraction-free sample preparation to HPV-LAMP, a test being developed for cervical cancer screening, and achieved good analytical performance (30.4 HPV16 positive cells, 7.6 HPV18 positive cells, andhttps://riceuniversity.zoom.us/j/94882434656?pwd=JSDpS9UFAroXz0yoYgdKOezATjx20r.1 (https://riceuniversity.zoom.us/j/94882434656?pwd=JSDpS9UFAroXz0yoYgdKOezATjx20r.1)

From Rice University - Open to the Public

item.title: "Còn nước, còn tát": The Ebbs and Flows of Water across Vietnamese Anglophone Cultural Production
item.description:
Thursday, July 2, 2026 at 6 PM

Location: Herring Hall

  Speaker: Karen Siu Doctoral Candidate Thesis Defense Department: English Location: Herring Hall 255 As a child and grandchild of Vietnamese refugees, my scholarship is personal and political, purposely following feminist and Ethnic Studies methodologies. It intertwines oral histories, creative nonfiction, and art with academic writing. This project's hybrid form is meant to be an innovation and contribution to the field of Vietnamese American Studies, which has often separated the genres of creative and academic writing rather than intertwining them. I ask, why water remains such an enduring symbol for Vietnamese diasporic writers, scholars, and artists, when it is often a painful reminder of war. When the capital of South Việt Nam, Sài Gòn, fell to the North Vietnamese Army, effectively ending the Việt Nam War on April 30, 1975, the date became known as ngày mất nước, the day we lost the country, we lost the water. In Vietnamese, nước (pronounced nyúk or \nü-äk\) means water, homeland, country, or nation. Fifty years after the war, it is clear that war and colonialism devastated the environment of Việt Nam, but what does it mean when the identity of the land, the water, and its people is also devastated? What does it mean to lose the water? This project pursues these questions at this poignant time. I examine the beginnings of nước in Vietnamese folklore alongside Vietnamese Anglophone Cultural Production, including literature, art, and war memorials and memorializations, in order to trace the transformation of nước before and after the Việt Nam War. I claim that Vietnamese Anglophone cultural works share an intimacy with water, deviating from a nước associated with the Vietnam War and towards a nước that instead emphasizes how our community survives and resists. I put forward nước as a cultural framework that is feminist, antiwar, and decolonial. Ultimately, I argue that these archives offer an ecofeminist, transcorporeal vision of hope and resilience in the wake of war, imperialism, and colonialism that gestures back to and expands on precolonial conceptions of nước. By ecofeminist and transcorporeal, I aim to show the fluidity and interconnectedness between gendered, reproductive bodies and water challenges the tendency to dichotomize humans and nature.   https://riceuniversity.zoom.us/j/99222333231?pwd=4PxBl3d0j2kfAz4vioBaDGTqZ23w1p.1 (Department : English)

item.title: Novel Computational Representations of TCRs and pMHCs Incorporating Structural Information for Translational Applications
item.description:
Friday, July 3, 2026 at 2 PM

Location: Duncan Hall

  Speaker: Jared Slone Doctoral Candidate Thesis Defense Department: Computer Science Location: Duncan Hall 1049 The ability to accurately predict TCR-pHLA binding specificity would have major impacts on infectious disease research, autoimmunity, and personalized cancer immunotherapy. Experimental characterization of TCR-pHLA binding specificity is labor-intensive and costly, however, and, despite significant advances in machine learning, computationally predicting TCR binding specificity remains difficult. This difficulty arises from both the immense combinatorial space of possible TCR-pHLA pairings and the fact that the proteins in question possess high degrees of freedom, making it difficult to model their physical interactions. This thesis advances the computational characterization of TCR-pHLA interactions through the development of novel computational methods for modeling, representing, and predicting the behavior of TCR-pHLA pairs.   Central to this thesis is the use of three-dimensional structural information when predicting TCR-pHLA binding specificity. First, this thesis introduces STAG, a graph convolutional neural network for predicting TCR-pHLA binding specificity from modeled protein structures. STAG incorporates geometric and physicochemical information into a novel graph representation of TCR-pHLA complexes. The results from this work demonstrate that using holistic structural representations of the proteins as input outperforms prior structure-based approaches for binding prediction which were based on interprotein contacts. This thesis next presents STAG-LLM, a multimodal machine learning architecture that integrates protein language models with structure-based geometric learning. By combining amino acid sequence-derived embeddings with graph representations of three-dimensional protein structure, STAG-LLM achieves improved predictive performance and increased data efficiency compared to approaches that use sequence or structure alone. Recognizing that TCR-pHLA interactions are inherently dynamic, this thesis further introduces STEGG, a computational framework for generating structural ensembles of TCR-pHLA complexes. Through domain-specific conformational sampling and optimization techniques, STEGG efficiently explores the conformational landscape of TCR-pHLA interactions and generates diverse conformational ensembles without the computational expense of molecular dynamics simulations. Collectively, these contributions establish new computational paradigms for computationally representing TCR-pMHC structures, modeling their flexibility, and predicting their interactions. More broadly, this thesis demonstrates advances in structural bioinformatics that improve the computational characterization of TCR-pHLA interactions and aid in the development of personalized immunotherapies. (Department : Computer Science)

item.title: Real and reciprocal space topology in rare earth intermetallics
item.description:
Friday, July 3, 2026 at 3 PM

Location: Brockman Hall for Physics

  Speaker: Kevin Allen Doctoral Candidate Thesis Defense Department: Physics & Astronomy Location: Brockman Hall for Physics 200 Topology in magnetic materials manifests across both real and reciprocal space, encompassing exotic band crossings in momentum space and nontrivial spin textures in real space. In strongly correlated and rare earth systems, these two aspects of topology are intertwined through the interplay of magnetic order, spin-orbit coupling, and electronic structure, giving rise to emergent phenomena including large anomalous Hall responses, extremely large magnetoresistance, and field-tunable soliton excitations. This dissertation presents four projects unified by this theme, spanning magnetic topological semimetals, atomically-sharp spin textures, strongly correlated heavy fermions, and spin-polarized surface catalysis. The first project identifies EuGa4 as a magnetic Weyl nodal ring semimetal, in which mirror symmetry protection stabilizes closed nodal line rings near the Fermi level in the spin-polarized state. Using angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES), quantum oscillation (QO) measurements, and density functional theory (DFT), we characterize the Weyl nodal ring states and their Landau quantization. The associated high carrier mobility gives rise to a transverse magnetoresistance ex- ceeding 200, 000% at 2 K and 14 T, more than two orders of magnitude large than any previously known magnetic topological semimetal, with non-saturating behavior up to 40 T explained by a theoretical model rooted in the nodal ring electronic structure. The second project demonstrates that the square-net rare earth compound EuRhAl4Si2 hosts atomically-sharp, field-tunable one-dimensional magnetic solitons stabilized by the competition between frustrated Ruderman-Kittel-Kasuya-Yosida (RKKY) exchange and uniaxial magnetic anisotropy. Magnetization measurements reveal a robust 1/3 magnetization plateau with fine steps corresponding to discrete soliton insertions, confirmed by anisotropic magnetotransport on a Focus ion beam (FIB)-fabricated microstructure. Neutron diffraction establishes the collinear ferrimagnetic ↑↑↓ ground state, while magnetic force microscopy (MFM) directly images the real space soliton lattice. First-principles exchange parameters and an effective J1–J2–K Heisenberg model, supplemented by Monte Carlo simulations, reproduce the observed field evolution and identify the soliton defects as the fundamental excitation. We further explore related square-net compounds by studying the series RTM4M'2 (R = Eu, Gd; T = Rh, Ir; M = Al, Ga; M' = Si, Ge), to identify additional candidates for hosting magnetic solitons and examine how structural and chemical tuning controls their stability. The third project reports a large anomalous Hall conductivity of approximately 1.6×104 Ω−1cm−1 in the centrosymmetric Kondo lattice compound Ce3Al11, a moderate heavy fermion with Kondo temperature TK = 10 K and a Sommerfeld coefficient of γ ≈ 100 mJ/molK2. The anomalous Hall effect (AHE) is stricly confined to the incommensurate antiferromagnetic phase below TN , and its temperature dependence reveals a crossover from extrinsic skew scattering near TN to an intrinsic Berry curvature dominated response at low temperature, consistent with the progressive onset of Kondo coherence. We attribute the symmetry breaking necessary to generate finite Berry curvature to the combination of incommensurate magnetic order and a Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interaction at the noncentrosymmetric CeII site establishing a route to large anomalous Hall responses in heavy fermion antiferromagnets without structural chirality or band inversion. The fourth project investigates how ferromagnetism in L10-CoPt enhances oxygen surface binding relevant to the oxygen reduction reaction. DFT calculations reveal than spin-polarized Co-d states, through exchange splitting of the minority spin channel toward the Fermi level, strengthen hybridization with O-p states and enhance adsorption energies by approximately 0.1 eV per oxygen atom relative to the non- magnetic case. By systematically varying the Pt overlayer thickness, we demonstrate that Co proximity to the surface amplifies these magnetic exchange effects, while the antiparallel oxygen spin configuration consistently yields the strongest binding. These results refine the d-band center model to incorporate spin-dependent hybridization and suggest pathways for optimizing catalytic performance through control of magnetic and structural degrees of freedom.https://riceuniversity.zoom.us/j/8841232928?pwd=Rys1Vm1TQ0RSR2twT1RiQkNzRDN2QT09 (https://riceuniversity.zoom.us/j/8841232928?pwd=Rys1Vm1TQ0RSR2twT1RiQkNzRDN2QT09) (Department : Physics & Astronomy)

item.title: Red, White & Bloom
item.description:
Saturday, July 4, 2026 at 8 PM

Location: McGovern Centennial Gardens, 1500 Hermann Drive, Houston, TX, 77004, United States

Celebrate the Fourth of July surrounded by beautiful blooms, festive fun, and one of Houston’s most beloved fireworks displays! Join us for “Red, White & Bloom” on Saturday, July 4, from 8 – 11 p.m. in the McGovern Centennial Gardens for a fantastic summer evening. This year is extra special as we mark America’s 250th birthday with a celebration packed with birthday party vibes! Enjoy free treats, family-friendly activities, music, lawn games, and more before the night wraps up with a spectacular view of the fireworks show from Miller Outdoor Theatre. Best of all, Red, White & Bloom is FREE and open to the public! Bring a blanket, gather your friends and family, and stroll through the vibrant garden spaces before settling in for the show. Spend Independence Day in this iconic spot, under the stars—a perfect setting for summer memories! The fireworks show at Miller Outdoor Theatre is set to start at approximately 9:45 p.m. The complimentary food items are while supplies last. Additional food vendors will be offering items for purchase by Miller Outdoor Theatre.

From Hermann Park Conservancy

Event details

item.title: Red, White & Cruise
item.description:
Saturday, July 4, 2026 at 8:30 PM

Location: Lake Plaza

Join us for Red, White & Cruise where you can watch Miller Outdoor Theatre’s fireworks show light up the sky from the peaceful waters of Hermann Park, with a special pedal boat rental from 8:30–10:30 p.m. on Saturday, July 4! As we celebrate 250 years of American independence, gather your crew and watch the sky come alive with fireworks while cruising across the lake in your own pedal boat under the stars. Saturday, July 4 8:30 – 10:30 p.m.  🎟️ $150 per boat (up to 4 people) includes: 🕘 Boat rental up to 2 hours 🍿 Drinks & snacks 🎊 Festive accessories 🅿️ Reserved parking in Lot I near Lake Plaza ⛵ Board anytime between 8:30–9:30 p.m. and enjoy the show from the water (fireworks begin at approximately 9:45 p.m., boats must return once fireworks finish) BOOK YOUR BOAT HERE!  🚨 Important Info: One rider per boat must be 18+ All riders 18+ must sign a digital waiver before boarding At least one rider must be 18+, with a physical government ID, and sign a waiver before boarding Tickets are non-refundable Event is rain or shine per Miller Outdoor Theatre’s plan In the event of lightning, pedal boats will close, and refunds will be issued  The purchase of a ticket includes an up-to 2-hour pedal boat ride for up to 4 people to enjoy the sight of Miller Outdoor Theatre’s fireworks show, drinks, snacks, festive treats, and a reserved parking spot in Lot I near Lott Hall by Lake Plaza (starting at 8 p.m.). Only one ticket purchase of $150 is needed for a 4-person boat (i.e. it is the cost per boat, not per rider).  A reminder that at least one rider per boat must be 18 years or older.  All riders 18 years or older must sign a digital waiver before boarding. Tickets are non-refundable. We are following Miller Outdoor Theatre’s plan for their event and fireworks show which is rain or shine. If there is lightning in the area, we will close the pedal boats and ticket holders will receive a refund.  Please arrive early to allow time to park in Lot I as roads in the Park will be closed by HPD and closures are subject to change based on their purview.  

From Hermann Park Conservancy

item.title: Pitch in at Hermann Park
item.description:
Wednesday, July 8, 2026 at 8:30 AM

Location: Lake Plaza

Give the Park you love a little extra care, and help us get ready for the world! Join us for “Pitch in at Hermann Park,” a hands-on volunteer series where community members of all ages come together to help care for Houston’s most cherished green spaces. We’re proud to work alongside FWC26Houston and Harris County–Houston Sports Authority in the Impact Houston 26 efforts to elevate the local environmental action on a global stage and build a lasting, positive legacy for our city. You’ll work alongside Conservancy staff on simple but meaningful projects like mulching, planting, and litter cleanup — the kind of small efforts that make a big impact for visitors, wildlife, and the health of the Park. This volunteer session will be in the Heart of the Park (6104 Hermann Park Drive, Houston Texas 77030). Volunteers will meet at the Pedal Boat Kiosk. Enter through Lot G (near the Zoo) or Lot E and park in Lot F.  Walk up the concrete sidewalk to the Pedal Boat Kiosk, and we will be watching for your group. No experience is needed — just comfortable clothes, closed-toe shoes, and a willingness to help. We’ll provide the tools, guidance, and a great way to spend your morning outdoors while meeting new people and giving back to the community. Whether you come with friends, family, coworkers, or on your own, your time truly matters here. Help us welcome the world to Hermann Park! SIGN UP HERE. Weather:   Volunteer projects are rain or shine, unless there is severe weather. Cancellations due to severe weather will be sent via text/email at least 1 hour before your shift. Use your best judgment when traveling in inclement weather. Questions? Email volunteer@hermannpark.org or call 713-524-5876. Remember, we are often out in the field, so email is the preferred method of getting in touch!

From Hermann Park Conservancy

item.title: K-Nexus: Hierarchical Gene-to-Cell AI Modeling for Perturbation-Induced Transcriptomic Shift Recovery
item.description:
Wednesday, July 8, 2026 at 5 PM

  Speaker: Zihan Zhao Masters Candidate Thesis Defense Department: Electrical and Computer Engineering Predicting cellular responses to genetic and chemical perturbations can accelerate therapeutic discovery by reducing experimental screening. However, predicting post-perturbation gene expression, particularly for unseen perturbations, remains challenging because response signals are sparse, cellular responses are heterogeneous and often unpaired, and responsive genes are obscured by dominant non-responsive genes. We propose K-Nexus, a hierarchical gene-to-cell framework that formulates perturbation response prediction as distribution-shift recovery over responsive genes. K-Nexus encodes perturbation-conditioned regulatory interactions at the gene level and aggregates them into cell-level representations that preserve response heterogeneity. Attention-based gene importance, response-aware weighting, and group-based distributional training focus learning on genes driving perturbation-induced changes. K-Nexus also incorporates perturbation and regulatory graphs to capture functional relationships between targeted genes and downstream pathways. Across three single-cell perturbation benchmarks, K-Nexus outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, particularly in responsive-gene distributional alignment, demonstrating improved recovery of expression shifts and generalization to unseen perturbations.   (Department : Electrical and Computer Engineering)

From Rice University - Open to the Public

item.title: Programmable control of small non-coding RNA expression in mammalian cells with synthetic RNA polymerase III transactivators.
item.description:
Friday, July 10, 2026 at 8 PM

Location: Keck Hall

  Speaker: Matthew Demelo Doctoral Candidate Thesis Defense Department: BioSciences-Biochemistry and Cell Biology Location: Keck Hall 102 Reliable control of gene expression via synthetic genetic circuits is essential to develop next-generation therapies, to facilitate efficient bioindustrial processes, and to enable advanced methods in basic cell biology. Synthetic control of small non-coding RNAs (sncRNAs) is of immense interest due to their important roles in development and disease in mammals, as well as their central role in technologies such as CRISPR/Cas gene editing and RNA interference. RNA polymerase III (Pol III) is the canonical polymerase for sncRNA expression, but few tools for regulated Pol III expression exist, requiring expression from RNA polymerase II (Pol II) promoters. While there has been extensive development of synthetic regulatory methods for expressing protein-coding RNAs and large non-coding RNAs via Pol II, these methods are often ineffective for robust expression of functional sncRNAs and often require post-transcriptional processing for sncRNAs to achieve functionality, impairing the feasibility of translating sncRNA technologies into medical and industrial applications. Here, I develop a toolkit capable of highly programmable and multiplexable expression of synthetic sncRNAs using Synthetic Pol III Transcription Factors (Syn3TFs), facilitating far more effective and robust expression of sncRNAs in mammals than previous methods. Additionally, I outline concise design rules for the construction of minimal Pol III promoters and complementary Syn3TFs, facilitating seamless construction of orthogonal Pol III circuits with customizable outputs. Finally, I demonstrate the utility of Syn3TFs in performing complex genetic logic and acting as efficient sensors for continuous molecular recording of cellular events. This platform represents a crucial first step to expanding the arsenal of circuit construction strategies for sncRNA expression and provides a strong foundation for continued development of Pol III-specific synthetic regulators.   (Department : BioSciences-Biochemistry and Cell Biology)

From Rice University - Open to the Public

item.title: Second Saturday Bird Survey
item.description:
Saturday, July 11, 2026 at 8 AM

2nd Saturday of the month8:00 – 10:00 amFree, but donations appreciatedAll ages, minors must be accompanied by an adult Meet in front of the Nature Center building Work on your bird watching skills while helping the Arboretum monitor bird populations on a fun, relaxed morning walk. Join us on the second Saturday of each month in the courtyard behind the building for a two-hour bird survey led by Arboretum staff members, Kelsey Low and Theo Ostler. We welcome all levels of bird watching skill and all ages (as long as you’re quiet) – but no dogs, please! If you have binoculars of your own, please bring them. We have a limited number of binoculars available to borrow. The June 13, 2026 Bird Survey has been cancelled. Please join us in July!

From Houston Arboretum & Nature Center

Event details

item.title: Blood Drive
item.description:
Saturday, July 11, 2026 at 10 AM

The Houston Arboretum is partnering with the Gulf Coast Regional Blood Center to host regular blood drives at the Arboretum. Click a date below to sign up to donate blood. All types of blood are needed to support the needs of our city. All successful donors will receive a voucher for a free Arboretum Individual Membership! 2026 january 3 february 7 March 7 April 4 may 2 june 13 july 11 august 1 september 26 october 3 november 7 december 12 If all time slots are grayed out, there are no more appointments available for that day. If you are coming to donate, please use the 610 Entrance for easiest access to the Donor Coach. Questions? Donor AdvocatesDo you have questions about your eligibility to give blood? Need more information about a test result? Please contact us with any medical questions you may have before, during or after your donation.  Donor Advocates team is available:  8 a.m. to 4 p.m. Monday–Friday  Email: donoradvocacy@giveblood.org | Call: (713) 791-6608 The Blood Center will respond to your email within 24 hours during regular business hours. You may also check the Eligibility Page for basic eligibility questions: https://www.giveblood.org/donate/check-eligibility/ Customer ExperienceDo you need to change your appointment? Have a question about our Donor Rewards program? Can’t find your Donor ID? Customer Experience team is available:  8 a.m. to 4 p.m. Monday–FridayEmail: cs@giveblood.org | Call: (713) 791-6373 or +1 (877) 948-5433 toll-freeThe Blood Center will respond to your email within 24 hours during regular business hours. You may also email Alyssa Giraldo at agiraldo@giveblood.org to reschedule your appointment.

From Houston Arboretum & Nature Center

item.title: Earlybirds Club
item.description:
Saturday, July 11, 2026 at 6 PM

Location: White Oak Music Hall - Downstairs, 2915 N Main St, Houston, TX

Doors: 6pm Earlybirds Club is a dance party for women and gender expansive folks that starts at 6pm and ends at 10pm ? because we have sh*t to do in the mornin DJ Caribbeats spins the songs that live in your bones: 80s, 90s, and 2000s pop, hip-hop, new wave, and R&B. But Earlybirds Club is more than a night out. It?s a permission slip to take up space. To be loud, weird, wild, sweaty, sparkly, sentimental, and completely yourself. It?s a judgment-free dance floor built on joy, kindness, connection, and the shared understanding that sensible footwear is a love language. And because joy is even better when it gives back, 10% of ticket proceeds go to the Susie Lee Legacy Fund, supporting nonprofits that uplift women, gender-expansive people, and communities who need it most. This show is 21+ All General Admission Tickets are Standing Room only IMPORTANT: WHITE OAK MUSIC HALL IS A CASHLESS VENUE. Please be sure to bring a debit or credit card. item.title: Introduction to Teaching the Holocaust
item.description:
Tuesday, July 14, 2026 at 9 AM

Location: Holocaust Museum Houston Classroom

Join Holocaust Museum Houston for a one day workshop designed to help educators to feel comfortable teaching about the Holocaust. The day will include a tour of the museum’s Holocaust Gallery, a talk from a Holocaust survivor or descendant, best practices for teaching about the Holocaust, an overview of some of the free resources available, and more.  Breakfast and lunch will be provided. Teachers will earn 6 CPE hours and 6 GT update hours. Teachers from the 6th-12th grade, in all subject areas, are encouraged to participate in this FREE workshop. Sign up through the registration link below. If you have any questions about the workshop, contact us as education@hmh.org.    Interested in a more in-depth exploration of the Holocaust? We also offer the Max M. Kaplan Summer Institute for Educators, which includes pedagogical and content sessions from scholars and museum educators. You can find more information and apply here: https://hmh.org/education/professional-development/max-m-kaplan-summer-institute/. Register Here

From Holocaust Museum Houston

item.title: Educating Traumatized Teenagers: The Extraordinary Story of a Swedish International School
item.description:
Thursday, July 16, 2026 at 6:30 PM

After World War II, Sweden absorbed and provided medical treatment for thousands of sick survivors. What to do about those who were teenagers, who had missed years of study on account of the war?  In September 1945, the Swedish government issued an order for schooling to be offered to those under the age of 18. In this presentation, Dr. Bernice Lerner will describe the complex process of recruiting wary adolescents, the challenges of teaching homeless, orphaned, and “environmentally damaged” teenagers, and instructors’ goals and methods at the most experimental of sites—the “Internat” school her mother attended. Dr. Bernice Lerner is the author of All the Horrors of War: A Jewish Girl, a British Doctor, and the Liberation of Bergen-Belsen, and other writings on the Holocaust and on virtue ethics. She is the former dean of adult learning at Hebrew College, former lecturer on the Holocaust at Boston University, and a senior scholar at Boston University’s Center for Character and Social Responsibility. Gerald S. Kaplan Endowed Lecture.

From Holocaust Museum Houston

Event details

item.title: Summer Camp All Grown Up
item.description:
Thursday, July 16, 2026 at 6:30 PM

Why should kids have all the fun? Treat yourself to all the joys of summer camp, but for grown-ups! Unwind after a stressful day with hands-on nature activities, crafts, and outdoor exploration, PLUS a simple snack and a refreshing beer from Saint Arnold Brewing Company. Please dress for the weather and wear walking shoes. $22 member / $37 nonmember per classThursday, June 18 and July 16Ages 21+ Micro World Thursday, June 18 There’s a whole world of activity going on under our noses. Using hand lenses and microscopes, we’ll hunt for the tiny creatures that make our ecosystems function, from roly-polies to plankton. We’ll even create a miniature world to take home: a micro-terrarium! register online CSI Nature Thursday, July 16 Nature is full of mysteries, and you can find clues everywhere if you know what to look for. Learn about signs of wildlife activity like tracks and scat, make your own gourmet chocolate “scat snacks,” and search the trails for evidence of the Arboretum’s “usual suspects.” register online

From Houston Arboretum & Nature Center

item.title: Cobalt Photocatalysis as a Platform for Tunable Hydrogen Atom Transfer in the Ring Opening of Cyclic Ethers
item.description:
Friday, July 17, 2026 at 8 PM

Location: BioScience Research Collaborative

  Speaker: Brian Funk Doctoral Candidate Thesis Defense Department: Chemistry Location: BioScience Research Collaborative 1060 A/B This dissertation explores thoughtful design and development of synthetic methods photocatalyzed by Vitamin B12 and analogous cobaloximes. Central to these protocols is control over diverging HAT from Earth-abundant Cobalt complexes and simple organic thiols, shown to be heavily influenced by proticity of the reaction environment. Through strategic control of HAT directionality, these works provide mild, complementary reactive scaffolds to classical hydrogenation by hydride nucleophiles and carbonyl production by traditional Meinwald rearrangements. The addition of these new techniques to the synthetic chemist's toolbox has the potential to improve the limited accessibility of hydride-like reactivity in chemical synthesis, influence the perception of cyclic ethers as precursors to valuable functional handles, and further advance the onset of VB12 and its derivatives as powerful photocatalysts. Armed with the strong mechanistic foundation elucidated throughout this dissertation, further application of Cobalt photocatalysis in broader substrate classes can maximize the impact of this powerful, sustainable, and tunable reactivity.   (Department : Chemistry)

From Rice University - Open to the Public

item.title: Cynthia Lee Fontaine Presents: Drag Brunch at the Punch
item.description:
Saturday, July 18, 2026 at 12 PM

Location: Punch Line Houston, 1204 Caroline Street, Houston, TX

Looking for the ultimate Houston drag brunch experience? Whether you're planning a bachelorette party, a birthday celebration, or just want a daytime drag show without the late-night crowd, this is your event. Drag Brunch at The Punch is a monthly extravaganza featuring fierce performances, sassy audience games, and unforgettable live drag performances ? paired with brunch cocktails, mimosas, and bites. The perfect daytime LGBTQ event for anyone who loves fabulous entertainment before sundown. Produced by RuPaul's Drag Race fan-favorite Cynthia Lee Fontaine, each show features Houston drag queens bringing show-stopping numbers, big laughs, and serious talent. Perfect for girls' day out, gay brunch, Pride celebrations, and anyone looking for a fun Saturday activity in Houston. No late nights required ? just good drinks, great queens, and the best drag brunch ticket in town. Grab your tickets now. Please note that Cynthia will not be making an appearance. IMPORTANT TIMES: 10:30 AM BOX OFFICE OPENS SEATING BEGINS AT 11:00AM - SHOWTIME IS AT 12:00PM This show is 18 & over with valid, government-issued photo ID. item.title: Opossums & Popsicles
item.description:
Saturday, July 18, 2026 at 6:30 PM

$37 member / $52 nonmember / $22 children ages 5-12Instructor: Wildlife Center of TexasAges 5+ Chill out with some of the coolest critters around! Grab a frozen treat, enjoy a refreshing root beer (or regular beer) from Saint Arnold Brewing Company, and get up close and personal with live wild animals from Wildlife Center of Texas. You’ll learn all about Houston’s amazing urban wildlife – including opossums – and then you’ll go on a guided hike to enjoy a truly wild night out. Please dress for the weather and wear walking shoes Register online Instructor Bio: The Wildlife Center of Texas is one of the largest rehabilitation centers in the country, admitting nearly 10,000 wild patients each year. Its mission is to care for all injured, orphaned, ill, and oiled wildlife through wildlife rehabilitation, public education, and oiled wildlife response.

From Houston Arboretum & Nature Center

item.title: Punk Rock Garage Sale
item.description:
Sunday, July 19, 2026 at 2 PM

Location: Bad Astronaut Brewery, 1519 Fulton Street, Houston, TX, 77009, United States

Insomnia Gallery and Deep End Records are excited to announce the date for our July Punk Rock Garage Sale! If you haven’t caught on yet, these happen on the 3rd Sunday of EVERY MONTH at Bad Astronaut brewing! While summer is heating up, stay relatively cool as this event is ***ALL INDOORS!*** and the event space itself now has AC! There will be OVER 50 VENDORS inside this massive adult playground of a venue which features pool tables, foosball, air hockey, cornhole and more (and all FREE to play!) We’ve got makers, artists, collectibles, thrift and vintage, music, books, video games, movies and vendors selling so much more! If you’re looking for a gift or to treat yourself, come to Insomnia Gallery’s Punk Rock Garage Sale for a true chance to score! NEW VENDORS EVERY MONTH! We’ll mix in a few pro vendors and artists as well to bring you a truly unique market each and every month! About this event:Based on the punk rock garage sales that took place regularly at Walter’s Downtown, Insomnia Gallery has been keeping this tradition alive for the last 6 years! DIY Forever! Sale hours: 2:00pm – 6:00pm Location: Bad Astronaut Brewing at 1519 Fulton FREE street parking on Sunday in addition to their back parking lot and grass parking lot! ** INTERESTED IN SETTING UP? Contact deependhtx@gmail.com with a general description of the items you’d like to sell and we’ll get back with you asap! Table fee ranges from $15-$25 depending on setup size!

From Bad Astronaut Brewery

item.title: INPRINT BOOK CLUB DISCUSSES SON OF NOBODY
item.description:
Sunday, July 19, 2026 at 4 PM

This Inprint Book Club meeting will take place via Zoom. The Zoom link will be the same for every meeting and will be provided in your Eventbrite email receipt (scroll down to the Additional Information section). On July 19, 2026 the Inprint Book Club will discuss the novel Son of Nobody by Yann Martel, who will appear as part of the 2025/2026 Inprint Margarett Root Brown Reading Series on Monday, April 27, 2026. Visit brazosbookstore.com to order your copy of Martel’s newest novel. Inprint Book Club meetings are free and open to the public and are facilitated by experienced book club leader Torie Ludwin. The full schedule of Inprint Book Club meetings for 2025/2026 season appears on the main Inprint Book Club webpage, which you can access by clicking here. If you have any further questions, please email info@inprint.org.

item.title: Animal Tales
item.description:
Monday, July 20, 2026 at 9:30 AM

Mondays, June 1 – August 39:30 am and 10:30 am $5 member / $7 nonmember (per child, per class)Ages 18 months to 3 years Each child must be accompanied by their guardian. Siblings are welcome to attend but must be registered to participate. Family Tree Membership level and above required for member discount. Join us for a weekly story time featuring some of our favorite animals! Each week we will read a nature-themed book, observe biofacts and/or live animals, and then venture out on the trails for a self-guided hike. Summer Children’s Program registration will open Tuesday, February 3, 2026 for Arboretum Members. Nonmember registration will open Tuesday, February 10, 2026. 9:30 am Classes June 1 – 9:30 am June 8 – 9:30 am June 15 – 9:30 am June 22 – 9:30 am June 29 – 9:30 am July 6 – 9:30 am July 13 – 9:30 am July 20 – 9:30 am July 27 – 9:30 am august 3 – 9:30 am 10:30 am Classes June 1 – 10:30 am June 8 – 10:30 am June 15 – 10:30 am June 22 – 10:30 am June 29 – 10:30 am July 6 – 10:30 am July 13 – 10:30 am July 20 – 10:30 am July 27 – 10:30 am august 3 – 10:30 am Please come dressed for the outdoors, including closed-toe shoes. faqs

item.title: Little Explorers
item.description:
Thursday, July 23, 2026 at 9:30 AM

Thursday June 4 – Aug 69:30 – 10:30 am11:00 am – 12:00 pm $10 member / $12 nonmember (per child, per class)Ages 2-4 Each child must be accompanied by their guardian. Siblings are welcome to attend but must be registered to participate. Family Tree Membership and above required for member discount. Our youngest naturalists are invited to join us for hands-on nature exploration. This program is a perfect way for our Little Explorers to discover more about the natural world around them. Please come dressed for the outdoors, including closed-toe shoes. A parent or guardian is required to attend program with children. Summer Children’s Program registration will open Tuesday, February 3, 2026 for Arboretum Members. item.title: Fourth Saturday Phenology Walk
item.description:
Saturday, July 25, 2026 at 9 AM

Fourth Saturday of the Month9:00 – 11:00 amFree, donations appreciatedAll ages, minors must be accompanied by an adult Meet in front of the Nature Center building Discover the hidden seasonal rhythms of nature firsthand on our guided Phenology Walks, where we track the life cycles of different plants across ecosystems, from the first spring bud burst to winter dormancy. You’ll learn how shifting weather patterns and climate change influence the timing of these important events in our local ecosystems and how you can become a community scientist, contributing vital data from your own backyard to a global research initiative. These relaxing morning walks are led by scientists from Rice University and staff from the Arboretum. We welcome nature enthusiasts of all ages, however kids 12 years and younger must be accompanied by a guardian. Learn more about the study of phenology and ways you can contribute at https://www.usanpn.org/nn Community Science Partner

From Houston Arboretum & Nature Center
